# Decisions log — Supabase integration

This file is the canonical at-a-glance record of design decisions reached during the shaping phase. Each entry is a settled decision; longer rationale lives in the linked component doc.

Decisions are grouped by area. The order within each group is rough chronological order of when the decision was reached, not priority order.

When a decision changes, **update this file**. When a new design hole surfaces, capture it in [`example/design-holes.md`](example/design-holes.md) until it resolves into a decision recorded here.

---

## Contract authoring — TypeScript surface

- **A1. No RLS capability flag.** Target presence is the gate. The Postgres target's `pack` carries RLS support; pack-aware typing makes the `.rls(...)` slot visible on `ContractModelBuilder` only when targeting Postgres. Non-Postgres targets do not see the method. (See [postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md), [`example/design-holes.md` #1](example/design-holes.md).)
- **A2. `.rls(...)` is a fourth named stage on the model builder**, alongside `.attributes(...)` and `.sql(...)`. Same staged-builder shape as the existing surface. Target-gated by the pack-aware typing described in A1. ([`example/design-holes.md` #2](example/design-holes.md))
- **A3. The argument is `Array<PolicyDescriptor>` — not a dict keyed by operation.** Each descriptor carries `{ name, operation, roles, using?, withCheck?, as? }`. The earlier dict-keyed shape was rejected on the basis that it made the TS surface *more* restricted than PSL (named-block policies), inverting the typical positioning. The array form restores TS as the more expressive surface. ([`example/design-holes.md` #2](example/design-holes.md))
- **A4. Multiplicity is lenient.** Multiple permissive policies for the same `(target, operation)` are valid. Postgres ORs them; the framework emits N CREATE POLICY statements. The TS surface validates that duplicate `name` within the same model is an error; it does not validate "one per op." ([`example/design-holes.md` #2](example/design-holes.md))
- **A5. `using` and `withCheck` accept `string | ((ctx) => string)`.** The function-form's `ctx` exposes one helper: `ref(modelHandle): string`, returning the canonical quoted namespace-qualified identifier (`"public"."profile"`, `"profile"` for `__unspecified__`, `"auth"."users"` for cross-contract). Subquery predicates that use `ref()` track renames automatically; bare-column predicates stay one-line strings. The verbatim escape hatch (a raw string with hardcoded names) remains available. ([`example/design-holes.md` #5](example/design-holes.md))
- **A6. Cross-contract `onDelete: 'cascade'` is permitted with no diagnostic.** The developer's explicit `cascade` at the call site is the audit trail. The repo-wide policy at [`.agents/rules/explicit-opt-in-over-diagnostics.mdc`](../../.agents/rules/explicit-opt-in-over-diagnostics.mdc) codifies the principle: explicit opt-ins are documentation; warnings on intentional paths are noise. ([cross-contract-refs spec](../cross-contract-refs/spec.md), [`example/design-holes.md` #3](example/design-holes.md))
- **A7. Composite / named uniques use `.attributes(...)`, not `.sql(...)`.** `.attributes(({ fields, constraints }) => ({ uniques: [constraints.unique([fields.x, fields.y], { name }) ] }))` is the canonical shape. Field-level `.unique({ name? })` handles single-column. No DSL extension was needed; the example app's earlier `uniqueConstraints` inside `.sql()` was wrong, not a missing feature. ([`example/design-holes.md` #4](example/design-holes.md))
- **A8. TS policy descriptor's `as` field is `'permissive' | 'restrictive'`, default `'permissive'`.** Mirrors PSL's B2 exactly so the two surfaces stay alignable. The default matches Postgres's `CREATE POLICY` default — users only type `as: 'restrictive'` when they want the rare combine-with-AND semantics. ([postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md))

## Contract authoring — PSL surface

- **B1. Policies are top-level named-block declarations:** `policy <name> { ... }`, scoped by the surrounding `namespace` block. Zero new grammar primitives — `<keyword> <ident> { body }` is existing PSL idiom. ([postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md))
- **B2. Body uses `key = value` lines** (the datasource/generator-style PSL body convention). Fields: `target = <Model>`, `operation = select|insert|update|delete|all`, `roles = [ident, ...]`, `using = "..."`, `withCheck = "..."`, `as = permissive|restrictive` (optional, default `permissive`). ([postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md))
- **B3. Multiplicity is lenient** — multiple permissive policies per `(target, operation)` are allowed when their PSL names differ. The framework emits N CREATE POLICY statements; Postgres ORs them. PSL and TS are aligned on this. ([postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md), [`example/design-holes.md` #2](example/design-holes.md))
- **B4. Cross-contract `target` is forbidden.** A policy can only attach to a model the contract owns. `target = supabase:auth.User` is a load-time error mirroring Postgres's permission model (you can't `CREATE POLICY` on a table you don't own). ([postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md))
- **B5. Predicates are verbatim strings in v0.1.** Authors type schema-qualified names matching their migrations. Renames in `target = ...` don't auto-track inside subquery predicates. Structured interpolation (`${ModelName}`, `${supabase:auth.User}`) is a **stretch goal**, not on the v0.1 critical path. ([postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md), [`example/design-holes.md` #5](example/design-holes.md))
- **B6. Namespace blocks are reopenable** (already settled in TML-2459); policies and models can live in separate PSL files within the same namespace; resolution joins them at load time. Duplicate policy names within `(namespace, target)` are a fail-fast load error. ([postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md))

## Cross-cutting

- **C1. Default policy name in PSL is not a concept** — block grammar requires a name in the head. There is no anonymous `policy { ... }` form. (Settled inline during PSL discussion; no separate doc.)
- **C2. `supabase()` callable-namespace shorthand dropped** (later fully superseded by [C6](#cross-cutting)). The original framing kept a `supabase` object with `.pack()` / `.contract<C>(json)` / `.roles` members while dropping the bare `supabase()` callable. C6 then went further and removed the umbrella object entirely in favour of subpath-only imports. The historical C2 record is retained for context; **see C6 for the canonical shape.** ([extension-supabase spec](../extension-supabase/spec.md), [`example/design-holes.md` #20](example/design-holes.md))
- **C3. Strict-vs-lenient positioning.** Across both surfaces the framework's typical stance is: **TS is the more expressive surface; PSL is the simpler restricted one.** Specific applications of this rule:
  - RLS policy multiplicity: both lenient (A4, B3) — Postgres-faithful.
  - Predicate interpolation: TS gets `ref()` in v0.1 (A5); PSL stays verbatim and gets interpolation only as a stretch (B5). This is the asymmetry the rule allows.
- **C4. Functions are not contract elements in v0.1.** The framework verifies declared contract elements; functions don't enter the contract. The four typical Supabase flows (FK to `auth.users` with cascade, server-generated UUID columns, RLS predicates using `auth.uid()` etc., column-default function invocations) map to existing mechanisms — cross-contract FK refs (A6), the framework's `DefaultFunctionRegistry`, and opaque RLS predicate strings (A5/B5). The verifier never introspects `pg_proc`. Missing functions surface as Postgres errors at migration / query time, which is acceptable.
  - The Supabase pack **may** register `auth.uid()` / `auth.jwt()` / `auth.role()` into `DefaultFunctionRegistry` if we decide to support them as column defaults (currently uncertain — flows that need it can fall back to the raw escape hatch). This is a small targeted extension of an existing mechanism; no new IR.
  - Promoting functions to first-class IR (with a `control` field, `pg_proc` verifier checks, planner DDL) stays out of v0.1 entirely; it pairs naturally with the trigger work as a stretch. Closes [`example/design-holes.md` #15](example/design-holes.md).
- **C5. Roles are first-class contract elements, resolved via aggregate — not via a `/contract` hand-export.** ✅ **Settled 2026-06-16** — confirmed by what cross-contract-refs and extension-supabase actually shipped.

  **Settled shape:**
  - Target-only `PostgresRole` IR class, parallel to `PostgresRlsPolicy`. The Supabase pack declares the standard role set (`anon`, `authenticated`, `service_role`) with `control: 'external'` (slice C — remaining work). App contracts may author their own roles in future.
  - PSL `roles = [authenticated]` identifiers resolve against the **loaded contract aggregate** (locally declared + extension-pack contributions). TS `.rls([...])` policy descriptors reference roles the same way — via the aggregate-aware surface, not imported constants.
  - **The `/contract` `roles`/`RoleRef` hand-export is dropped.** It reinvents contract access. In Prisma Next you reach a contract's models and roles through the contract you initialise the builder / ORM with (composed via `extensionPacks`). A bespoke per-role constant the extension hand-ships is not idiomatic and not needed. The shipped `/contract` subpath exports only `extensionModel`-branded model handles (`AuthUser`, `AuthIdentity`, `StorageBucket`, `StorageObject`) — confirmed by `packages/3-extensions/supabase/src/exports/contract.ts`.
  - **The `PostgresRole` IR + `pg_roles` verifier parts of this decision still stand.** Verifier introspects `pg_roles` under the framework's control-policy dispatch (see [ADR 224](../../docs/architecture%20docs/adrs/ADR%20224%20-%20Control%20Policy%20—%20framework-locked%20vocabulary%20and%20family-owned%20dispatch.md)): missing `external` roles fail loudly so policy creation errors don't surface only at migration time. This is slice C work.

  Evidence: cross-contract-refs shipped the brand machinery (live in `extensionModel`); extension-supabase `/contract` ships model handles only, no roles export. The re-examination note from 2026-06-05 is resolved.
- **C6. Extension package entrypoints are subpath-only:** `/pack`, `/contract`, `/runtime`. Each ships only what its name implies. No catch-all `extensionName.*` umbrella on the main package. Pack metadata is value-imported (`import supabasePack from '@prisma-next/extension-supabase/pack'`), not call-style. Mirrors existing convention in `packages/3-extensions/cipherstash/` and `packages/3-extensions/pgvector/`. Tree-shaking discipline is a hard rule: `/pack` must not transitively pull in runtime code; `/contract` must not transitively pull in SDK code. **Supersedes [C2](decisions.md)** — the entire `supabase.*` umbrella goes away, not just the `supabase.pack()` shorthand.
- **C7. Emitter-generated `contract-builder.ts` is a stretch goal, not v0.1.** Extension authors hand-write the `/contract` submodule for v0.1 (the Supabase package does so directly). The emitter today produces `contract.json` + `contract.d.ts`; extending it to also produce a typed authoring builder is roadmapped but not on the critical path. Closes design hole #17 (the consumer-side mapped-type machinery I had sketched isn't needed — extensions ship concrete pre-built types). See [`example/design-holes.md` #17](example/design-holes.md).
- **C8. Introspection is the authoring mechanism for extension contracts (roadmap, not v0.1).** Introspect a live canonical database (e.g., a fresh Supabase project) → emit a contract.json with `defaultControl: 'external'` → ship as the extension's source of truth. When upstream schemas evolve, re-introspect to refresh.
  - **Round-trip property as a test obligation:** introspect → emit → re-introspect → diff must be empty. Drift is either a real upstream schema change (refresh) or a framework IR regression that broke an extension's round-trip (fix). Either way it's the canonical signal.
  - The same pattern applies to any future extension describing an existing database (not just Supabase).
  - v0.1 ships **targeted** introspection for verifier purposes (`pg_roles` per C5, `pg_policies` per C10). Those code paths are written with the broader pattern in mind, not as one-offs. The full introspection-driven authoring pipeline is roadmapped.
  - Skill capturing the canonical extension shape (including this round-trip pattern) tracked as [TML-2492](https://linear.app/prisma-company/issue/TML-2492/skill-author-a-prisma-next-extension).
- **C9. RLS policy wire names are content-addressed.** Wire-level `policyname` in `pg_policies` has the form `<user_prefix>_<8 hex chars>`. The user prefix is what the author types (TS `name:` field; PSL `policy <name>` head identifier); the suffix is the first 8 hex chars of SHA-256 over the canonical content tuple `(canonical(using), canonical(withCheck), sort(roles), operation, as)`. Schema and table are excluded — they're orthogonal identity. The contract.json carries the **full wire name**; the user authors the prefix. Duplicate prefixes within `(schema, table)` are a lowering error. No version marker — the contract storage hash already signals normalizer changes (per ADR 004). Full design in [postgres-rls `specs/adr-content-addressed-policy-names.md`](../postgres-rls/specs/adr-content-addressed-policy-names.md).
- **C10. RLS verifier semantics build on C9.** Identity is the full wire name. Body equivalence is implicit in the suffix; the verifier never compares bodies for equivalence purposes. Three new target-side `SchemaIssue` kinds extend the union:
  - **`rls_policy_renamed`** — matching hash, different prefix → planner emits `ALTER POLICY ... RENAME TO`. Closes the "policy rename heuristic" working assumption: rename detection is no longer "deferred to v0.1; ship drop+create" — it's structurally free under C9.
  - **`rls_policy_tampered`** — introspected name's suffix doesn't match a freshly computed hash of the introspected body. Catches manual `ALTER POLICY` outside the framework. The one body-level check the verifier still performs.
  - **`rls_not_enabled`** — table has declared policies but `pg_class.relrowsecurity = false`. Drift signal; the planner auto-enables when policies are declared, so this should only fire after manual `DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`.
  - Missing policies → error under `managed`; extras → governed by the table's control policy. Predicate-equivalence noise that would have been the dominant v0.1 false-positive class is structurally eliminated. Closes [`example/design-holes.md` #19](example/design-holes.md).
- **C11. Implicit `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`.** Model-level `rls?: 'auto' | 'enabled' | 'disabled'`, default `'auto'` (infer from policy presence: any declared policy → enabled). `'enabled'` is the explicit defensive default for "RLS on, no policies yet" (denies all access). `'disabled'` is the explicit override for "this table never wants RLS" — useful for test fixtures and tables the framework deliberately leaves open. The planner emits `ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE/DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` based on the resolved state; the verifier checks `pg_class.relrowsecurity` matches. ([postgres-rls spec](../postgres-rls/spec.md))
- **C12. Runtime target-layer class introduced in v0.1; Supabase extends it.** ✅ **Shipped in PR #792 (TML-2502), 2026-06-10 — the as-built design differs materially from the sketch below; [ADR 230](../../docs/architecture%20docs/adrs/ADR%20230%20-%20Runtime%20target%20layer%20session-coupled%20connections.md) is the authoritative record.** (As built: `SqlRuntimeBase` abstract family seam + `*RuntimeImpl` concretions behind bare-name interfaces; `createRuntime` deleted; role binding via session-coupled connections with `set_config(...,false)` + `RESET ALL`, not the `execute()`-override/`SET LOCAL`-per-call sketch below.) The original sketch, preserved for the umbrella's decision history:
  1. **Export `SqlRuntime`** (rename `SqlRuntimeImpl`, mark public). Its existing `protected override lower(...)` / `protected override runDriver(...)` hooks were already designed for subclassing — only the export was missing.
  2. **Add `class PostgresRuntime extends SqlRuntime`** in the Postgres extension. Initially identity-like; exists as the target-layer extension point so future Postgres-specific runtime behaviour (COPY, LISTEN/NOTIFY, prepared-statement caching) has a structural home. The `postgres()` factory constructs a `PostgresRuntime` instead of going to `createRuntime`.
  3. **Add `class SupabaseRuntime extends PostgresRuntime`** in the Supabase extension. Overrides `execute()` (and the transaction-scoped pathways the role-bound `Db` uses) to wrap operations in `BEGIN; SET LOCAL role = ...; SET LOCAL request.jwt.claims = ...; …; COMMIT`. Inherits every other Postgres runtime behaviour transparently — when Postgres adds COPY or LISTEN/NOTIFY support to `PostgresRuntime`, Supabase users get it for free.

  This mirrors TML-2459's three-layer IR architecture at the runtime layer: framework abstract → family concrete → target concrete. Composition / decorator was considered and rejected — `SupabaseRuntime` genuinely IS-A `PostgresRuntime`, and explicit forwarding of every method would require code changes whenever the base runtime grew. The refactor is small (~50–100 LOC: one rename + one new export, one near-empty Postgres subclass, factory updated to use it) and bounded by the already-explicit `protected override` hooks on `RuntimeCore`. Captured in [runtime-target-layer `specs/adr-runtime-target-layer.md`](../../docs/architecture%20docs/adrs/ADR%20230%20-%20Runtime%20target%20layer%20session-coupled%20connections.md). Closes [`example/design-holes.md` #7](example/design-holes.md), [#8](example/design-holes.md), [#11](example/design-holes.md), [#13](example/design-holes.md), [#14](example/design-holes.md) — see the [extension-supabase spec](../extension-supabase/spec.md) §"Runtime facade" for the pinned shape.
- **C13. The integration is delivered against a walking skeleton.** A single runnable example app at `examples/supabase` is stood up early (by `extension-supabase` M1, which needs only the landed foundation + control-policy) and grown one feature at a time as each constituent lands. "Wire your feature into the running `examples/supabase` app" is a definition-of-done clause on every constituent, so cross-project seam mismatches surface continuously rather than at integration time. M1 ships `/pack` + the PSL-authored Supabase contract, and the skeleton runs on the stock `@prisma-next/postgres/runtime`; the Supabase `/runtime` subpath (role binding) is deferred to M2. **The `/contract` typed-handles subpath is not part of M1** (re-cut 2026-06-05) — no consumer in the skeleton; deferred to `cross-contract-refs` / `postgres-rls` (see C5; the handles export may not exist at all in its sketched form). The skeleton **does** prove the `external`-contract machinery end-to-end: it migrates the composed contract against a DB seeded with the `auth.*`/`storage.*` tables and asserts the planner emits no DDL for them and the verifier confirms them present. M1 therefore ships the **schemas + tables increment** of `bootstrapSupabaseShim` (the verifier fails on a missing external table); the roles + `auth.uid()`/`jwt()`/`role()` functions are deferred per C14. The example lives top-level (alongside the other example apps), not under the package; this **supersedes** the earlier `packages/3-extensions/supabase/examples/basic/` location in the older spec/plan drafts. Full strategy + growth table in the [umbrella README](README.md) §"Walking skeleton".
- **C14. Two-lane test strategy.** RLS, roles, and JWT-claim session vars are plain Postgres mechanisms, so the **hermetic lane** runs on **PGlite + a hand-authored Supabase shim** (`bootstrapSupabaseShim(client)`, mirroring [`test/integration/test/postgres-bootstrap.ts`](../../test/integration/test/postgres-bootstrap.ts)): seeds the `anon`/`authenticated`/`service_role`/`authenticator` roles, the `auth` schema + `auth.users`, and `auth.uid()`/`auth.jwt()`/`auth.role()` as SQL functions reading the session GUCs. No Docker; runs every PR. **The shim is grown incrementally (re-cut 2026-06-05):** `extension-supabase` **M1 authors the first increment** — `CREATE SCHEMA auth, storage` + the `auth.users`/`auth.identities`/`storage.buckets`/`storage.objects` tables — because M1's skeleton migrates the composed contract through the verifier, which **fails on a missing `external` table** (`framework-components/.../verifier-disposition.ts`), so the tables must exist. Later increments attach to the same fixture: `postgres-rls` adds the roles + `auth.uid()`/`jwt()`/`role()` functions (for policy enforcement); `cross-contract-refs` adds the FK *into* the already-seeded `auth.users` (it does not create the table). The roles + functions are **not** in M1 — M1 touches no RLS or role-binding. (An earlier 2026-06-05 draft deferred the whole shim; that was wrong — proving the package's core `external` claim requires the tables present, so the table increment belongs in M1.) The **acceptance lane** (manual / nightly, not per-PR) runs against real Supabase — Supabase CLI via the `supabase/setup-cli` GitHub Action + `supabase start`, or a cloud project behind secrets — and is the ground truth for GoTrue-issued JWTs and the [C8](decisions.md) round-trip property. Corollary on RLS sequencing: **policy correctness** (an emitted `CREATE POLICY` filters rows when a role is active) is testable now via manual `SET ROLE`, independent of the runtime project; **automatic role binding** (`asUser(jwt)` sets the role below middleware) and its live-query e2e arrive with [runtime-target-layer](../../docs/architecture%20docs/adrs/ADR%20230%20-%20Runtime%20target%20layer%20session-coupled%20connections.md) + `extension-supabase` M2.

- **C15. Cross-space *querying* is not built; admin access to extension-owned tables goes through the `service_role` facade entrypoint.** ✅ Settled 2026-06-16 (extension-supabase slice D).
  - **Boundary.** `cross-contract-refs` deliberately shipped the minimum: FK *references* across contract spaces, **no cross-space querying** and no mechanism to expose another contract's tables in the app contract's queryable surface. In the app's emitted contract, `auth.*` appears only as a cross-space FK-reference coordinate (domain plane), **not** as a queryable storage table. So `db.asUser(jwt).sql.auth.users` off the app db does not (and is not meant to) work — an earlier `overview.md` sketch showing that was wrong and has been corrected.
  - **Supabase permission reality.** Prisma Next connects over a **direct Postgres connection** (not PostgREST). Over that connection `service_role` has grants on the `auth` schema + `BYPASSRLS`, so it *can* read `auth.*`; `anon` / `authenticated` have **no** `auth.*` grants. (The widespread "you can't query `auth.users`" advice is about the PostgREST/`supabase-js` HTTP path, which excludes the `auth` schema — it does not apply to a direct connection.)
  - **The entrypoint — a secondary root, not a merge.** Admin access to Supabase-internal tables is exposed **only on the `service_role`-bound db**, as a **secondary root**: `db.asServiceRole().supabase.sql.auth.users` / `.orm.auth.AuthUser` (and `storage.*`). `asServiceRole().sql`/`.orm` stay app-contract-only; `asUser` / `asAnon` have no `.supabase` at all — binding the capability to the role that holds the grant. The `.supabase` facet is the extension contract's **own intact surface**: its own `ExecutionContext` + a second runtime bound to it, **sharing the app runtime's driver/pool + the `service_role` session**, with marker-verification off (the `external` extension contract owns no app-space marker). **The two `Contract`s are NOT merged** — a first attempt that merged them was rejected: the runtime is contract-bound by `storageHash` (`SqlFamilyAdapter.validatePlan` requires `plan.meta.storageHash === context.contract.storage.storageHash`), and merging also breaks codec-registry uniqueness and the marker check. Two intact contexts/runtimes sharing one pool is the sanctioned shape (the ADR 230 pattern). Preferred over making users hand-construct a second db.
  - **Caveat (docs, not enforced):** Supabase-internal schema can drift across platform upgrades; for user *management* prefer the GoTrue Admin API. Direct `service_role` SQL is for ad-hoc/admin reads.
  - **Future direction (interim → principled).** The two-runtimes-sharing-a-pool secondary root is the pragmatic interim. The principled end state is a single `Runtime` bound to the **aggregate contract** (composed app + extension spaces) serving all roots natively — and the substrate real cross-space querying would ride on. Deferred (see [`deferred.md`](deferred.md)).

- **C16. Brownfield `contract infer` in a Supabase environment must omit extension-owned elements.** ✅ Settled 2026-07-02 (scope decision). Adopting an existing Supabase app via `contract infer` must write a meaningful `contract.prisma` containing only the app's *own* schema (`managed`), omitting every element the Supabase extension owns (`auth`/`storage`/… tables, native enum types, roles) — those are supplied by `extensionPacks: [supabasePack]` (`external`). Today `contract infer` is neither control-policy- nor extension-aware: with no contract it introspects the default `public` schema and emits everything `managed`, blind to pack ownership; the pack's `external` policy plays no part in inference. Making it extension-aware — infer = introspected schema − what the aggregate contract already describes — is a **launch requirement** of this project ([Slice G](../extension-supabase/plan.md)), not a follow-up. This pulls the previously-deferred "introspection-based emit" and "`adopt --from-database`" items into scope. Ownership resolution is shaped (TML-2962, [slice spec](../extension-supabase/slices/g-extension-aware-infer/spec.md)): the family instance passes the stack packs' contract spaces as-is into the target `inferPslContract` hook; the inferrer skips elements the aggregate declares, matched by `(schemaName, tableName)` tree identity — so a pack's `auth.users` can never suppress an app's `public.users`, and pack-owned objects in shared schemas like `public` are omitted by the same test. The mechanism is independent of [Slice F](../extension-supabase/plan.md); F's complete contract only deepens what gets omitted.

## Architectural offcuts (deserve their own treatment, not RLS-specific)

These surfaced during RLS / Supabase discussions but apply to PSL or the framework as a whole. Each will be drafted as an ADR or architecture doc when the right project pulls them in.

- **OC1. Two body-form pattern in PSL.** Two distinct body-statement forms coexist:
  - `field Type @attrs...` for *typed elements with structure* (model fields, future "members").
  - `key = value` for *instance-level static properties / configuration* (datasource, generator, `policy`, future similar declarations).

  Both can coexist in the same block where it makes sense. The pattern deserves its own ADR independent of RLS. Draft target: alongside whichever project formalises the `policy` grammar.
- **OC2. Future `policyGroup` for shared-target policies.** A `policyGroup UserPolicies { target = User; policy ... { ... } }` form that hoists shared properties was sketched. Deferred until real Supabase contracts show the repetition pain in practice. Capture as a one-line "future direction" wherever the policy grammar is specified.
- **OC4. PSL `environment` block for ambient configuration.** Cluster-/database-level objects (roles today; potentially extensions, event triggers, publications, database settings) have no schema-scoped home. Slice C ships them as top-level `role` blocks whose entities carry the unbound coordinate — workable, but it overloads the unbound slot, whose real meaning is "a schema whose binding is late-resolved by search_path". A future `environment { role Anon; }` namespace-equivalent would give ambient declarations an explicit lexical home, disentangle "ambient object" from the late-binding schema slot, and open a path to *managed* ambient objects (a planner that owns `CREATE EXTENSION` needs exactly this surface). Deferred indefinitely (2026-07-13); revisit when a second ambient entity kind arrives. Noted for spec time: the keyword ("environment" reads as deployment/env-var config to half the audience) and the member form (`role Anon;` statement form per OC1, leaving room for `role Admin { ... }` configuration bodies).
- **OC3. PSL `${...}` interpolation in string literals.** Required for the structured-reference equivalent of TS's `ref(model)`. New string-literal kind in the lexer; small but real parser change. Deferred from v0.1 (B5).
- **OC4. Content-addressed wire names for Postgres-normalized objects.** The naming pattern landed in C9/C10 for `PostgresRlsPolicy` is general. It applies anywhere `pg_*.<body_column>` is reprinted by Postgres's expression printer at storage time — **indexes** (`pg_indexes.indexdef`), **check constraints** (`pg_constraint.consrc`), **views** (`pg_views.definition`), **function bodies** (`pg_proc.prosrc`). The framework convention (8-hex SHA-256 suffix, canonical normalizer, prefix-only authoring) stays constant across applications; each per-kind backport is a focused project decision based on whether plain-name false-positive cost has become a real complaint for that object kind. Indexes are the highest-likely next target — DBA UX surface is wider (REINDEX, query plans, error messages), so when that project lands the "ugly suffix" trade-off needs explicit documentation. Captured here so future projects can reach for the pattern instead of reinventing it. ([postgres-rls `specs/adr-content-addressed-policy-names.md`](../postgres-rls/specs/adr-content-addressed-policy-names.md) § "Forward applicability".)

## Agent / tooling process changes (separate PR)

These are not project-shape decisions but came out of the discussion. They're staged in PR [#486](https://github.com/prisma/prisma-next/pull/486) (open at time of writing) so they can land independently of the Supabase project shaping.

- **D1. Research before asking.** Drive skills (`drive-discussion`, `drive-create-spec`, `drive-create-plan`) require codebase investigation before opening discussion threads or drafting specs. The agent should not ask the user about state the codebase can answer.
- **D2. Explicit opt-in over noisy diagnostics.** Repo-wide rule at [`.agents/rules/explicit-opt-in-over-diagnostics.mdc`](../../.agents/rules/explicit-opt-in-over-diagnostics.mdc). When a user choice has a non-obvious consequence, prefer requiring explicit opt-in at the call site over emitting a diagnostic on every build.

---

## Decisions still open

Active open work, by cluster:

- **Runtime cluster** — All closed.
  - #7 / #8 / #11 / #13 / #14 closed by C12 — shipped under ADR 230: `SupabaseRuntimeImpl` extends `PostgresRuntimeImpl` (which implements `PostgresRuntime`). Role binding uses **session-coupled connections** via `set_config('role'/'request.jwt.claims', …, false)` + `RESET ALL` on release (not `SET LOCAL` in a transaction — that sketch was superseded); `asUser(jwt)` is async (JWT validation via `jose`); `asAnon()`/`asServiceRole()` are sync; `middleware` forwarded to the base runtime; `transaction` uses `withTransaction` against a `RoleSession`. See [ADR 230](../../docs/architecture%20docs/adrs/ADR%20230%20-%20Runtime%20target%20layer%20session-coupled%20connections.md) for the as-built design.
- **IR cluster** — All closed.
  - #15 (Function IR) closed by C4 — functions are not v0.1 contract elements.
  - #17 (`TypedContract<T>` accessor surface) closed by C5+C6+C7 — extensions ship pre-built concrete `/contract` builders; no consumer-side mapped-type machinery needed.
- **Verifier cluster** — All closed.
  - #19 (RLS verifier check semantics) closed by C9 + C10 — content-addressed wire names make predicate-equivalence noise structurally impossible; verifier semantics built on top.

🟡 / 🟢 holes (#6, #9, #10, #12, #16, #18, #20) are default-able with working assumptions; resolve when implementation forces the question.
